name: Clytemnestra
description: Clytemnestra was the wife of Agamemnon, king of Mycenae, and sister to Helen of Troy. Her life was marked by tragedy and vengeance. Before the Trojan War, Agamemnon sacrificed their daughter, Iphigenia, to appease Artemis and ensure favorable winds for the Greek fleet. This act fueled Clytemnestra's burning hatred for her husband. While Agamemnon was away fighting in Troy for ten long years, Clytemnestra took Aegisthus as her lover and plotted her revenge. Upon Agamemnon's triumphant return, Clytemnestra, with Aegisthus's help, murdered him in his bath. She then ruled Mycenae with Aegisthus until her son, Orestes, returned to avenge his father's death and kill both Clytemnestra and Aegisthus. Clytemnestra is a complex figure, often portrayed as a strong, independent woman driven to violence by grief and injustice. She represents the destructive power of revenge and the consequences of betrayal, challenging traditional notions of wifely obedience and highlighting the cyclical nature of violence within the House of Atreus. Her story serves as a cautionary tale about the devastating effects of unchecked emotions and the enduring legacy of trauma.
subtitle: Queen of Mycenae, Driven by Revenge
achievements: Became a symbol of female rage

keyMyths.title: Sacrifice of Iphigenia
keyMyths.description: Agamemnon sacrificed his daughter, Iphigenia, to appease Artemis and allow the Greek fleet to sail to Troy. This act deeply scarred Clytemnestra and fueled her desire for revenge against her husband.

keyMyths.title: Murder of Agamemnon
keyMyths.description: Upon Agamemnon's return from Troy, Clytemnestra, with the help of her lover Aegisthus, murdered him in his bath. This act was driven by vengeance for Iphigenia's death and Agamemnon's infidelity.

keyMyths.title: Orestes' Revenge
keyMyths.description: Years later, Agamemnon and Clytemnestra's son, Orestes, returned to Mycenae and, urged by his sister Electra, killed Clytemnestra and Aegisthus to avenge his father's murder, fulfilling the cycle of violence.

sources.author: Aeschylus
sources.description: A trilogy of plays detailing the curse on the House of Atreus, including Agamemnon's murder and Orestes' revenge.
sources.text: A trilogy of plays detailing the curse on the House of Atreus, including Agamemnon's murder and Orestes' revenge.

sources.author: Euripides
sources.description: A play focusing on Electra's role in plotting Clytemnestra's death.
sources.text: A play focusing on Electra's role in plotting Clytemnestra's death.

sources.author: Homer
sources.description: Brief mentions of Clytemnestra's betrayal and Agamemnon's murder upon his return from Troy.
sources.text: Brief mentions of Clytemnestra's betrayal and Agamemnon's murder upon his return from Troy.

associations.name: Axe
associations.significance: Represents Clytemnestra's violent act of murdering Agamemnon and Cassandra. Symbolizes betrayal and revenge.

associations.name: Serpent
associations.significance: Often associated with Clytemnestra due to her cunning and vengeful nature, mirroring the serpent's hidden danger.

associations.name: Vengeance
associations.significance: The driving force behind Clytemnestra's actions, stemming from Agamemnon's sacrifice of Iphigenia.

associations.name: Mycenae
associations.significance: The location of Clytemnestra's palace and the stage for her tragic acts of betrayal and murder.

associations.name: Betrayal
associations.significance: Clytemnestra's act of murdering Agamemnon represents the ultimate betrayal of a wife against her husband.

associations.name: Curse of the House of Atreus
associations.significance: Clytemnestra's actions perpetuate the cycle of violence and retribution that plagues her family.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Like Clytemnestra, Gudrun seeks vengeance for the death of her loved ones, perpetuating a cycle of violence.
Medb, like Clytemnestra, is a powerful and assertive queen whose actions are driven by ambition and a desire for control.
Both women exhibit fierce determination and resourcefulness in the face of adversity, seeking justice and restoration.

worshipPractices.name: Hero Shrines
worshipPractices.description: While not worshipped as a goddess, Clytemnestra, as a figure of immense power and tragic fate, might have been honored at hero shrines. These local shrines were dedicated to figures from myth whose lives had a profound impact on the community. Libations and offerings may have been made to appease her spirit or seek understanding of her actions.

cultural.modernLegacy: Clytemnestra's story continues to resonate in modern literature, theater, and film, serving as a powerful exploration of themes such as revenge, betrayal, and the consequences of violence. Her character is often reinterpreted to reflect contemporary concerns about gender, power, and justice, cementing her status as a complex and enduring figure in Western culture.

extendedContent.title: Early Life and Betrayal
extendedContent.content: Clytemnestra was the daughter of King Tyndareus of Sparta and Leda. Her sister was Helen, whose abduction by Paris ignited the Trojan War. Clytemnestra's life was marked by tragedy early on when her first husband, Tantalus, and their infant son were murdered by Agamemnon. Agamemnon then took Clytemnestra as his wife, further complicating their relationship with a foundation of violence and loss. They had several children, including Iphigenia, Electra, Orestes and Chrysothemis. The sacrifice of Iphigenia was the breaking point. Agamemnon, to appease the goddess Artemis and ensure favorable winds for sailing to Troy, sacrificed their daughter. This act of ultimate paternal betrayal fueled Clytemnestra's resentment and set in motion her plans for revenge. The seeds of her later actions were sown in this early trauma, shaping her into a figure of formidable anger and determination.

extendedContent.title: The Reign of Terror
extendedContent.content: While Agamemnon was away fighting the Trojan War, Clytemnestra took Aegisthus as her lover, consolidating her power in Mycenae. Together, they ruled the kingdom, defying the traditional patriarchal structures of ancient Greece. Clytemnestra was not merely a passive figure; she was a shrewd and ambitious ruler, capable of maintaining control in a volatile political landscape. Her affair with Aegisthus provided her with the necessary support to challenge Agamemnon's authority and avenge Iphigenia's death. She skillfully manipulated the situation to her advantage, using her position as queen to undermine Agamemnon's power and prepare for his eventual demise. Her actions during Agamemnon's absence demonstrate her strength and cunning, solidifying her status as a formidable figure in Greek mythology. Upon Agamemnon's return, Clytemnestra, with Aegisthus' help, murdered him and his captive, Cassandra. This act was a culmination of years of pent-up anger and a calculated act of defiance against the man who had wronged her so deeply.

extendedContent.title: Legacy of Vengeance
extendedContent.content: Clytemnestra's act of vengeance had far-reaching consequences, perpetuating the cycle of violence within the House of Atreus. Her son, Orestes, driven by the command of Apollo and his sister Electra, eventually returned to Mycenae and killed Clytemnestra and Aegisthus to avenge his father's death. This matricide, while fulfilling a sense of justice for Agamemnon's murder, also carried a heavy burden of guilt and further complicated the family's already tragic history. Clytemnestra's story serves as a cautionary tale about the destructive nature of revenge and the enduring consequences of betrayal. Her actions continue to be debated and reinterpreted, solidifying her place as one of the most complex and compelling figures in Greek mythology. She represents the power of female rage, the complexities of familial relationships, and the enduring struggle for justice in a world often defined by violence and injustice.

shortDescription: Wife of Agamemnon who murdered him to avenge her daughter's sacrifice. A symbol of vengeance and female power.
